_note: Live wildlife cams as real places. Every webcam/window id verified is_live + playable_in_embed via yt-dlp on the generated date — same honesty rule as everywhere: a feed that rots removes its own tab at runtime. Sister picks already live in media.json: yellowstone, kruger-national-park, maasai-mara.

locations.name: Brooks Falls · Katmai
locations.blurb: Every summer the world's largest gathering of brown bears fishes the sockeye salmon run at this six-foot waterfall deep in Alaska's Katmai National Park. This is the most famous wildlife cam on Earth — Fat Bear Week is judged from these very frames.
locations.fun_fact: Katmai National Park has roughly 2,200 brown bears — far more bears than year-round human residents.

locations.name: Big Bear Eagle Nest
locations.blurb: Jackie and Shadow, Big Bear Valley's celebrity bald-eagle pair, keep their nest 145 feet up a Jeffrey pine above the lake. Millions tune in every nesting season to watch eggs, chicks, and mountain snowstorms roll through.
locations.fun_fact: Bald eagle nests are reused and enlarged year after year — some grow heavier than a small car.

locations.name: Decorah Eagles
locations.blurb: The nest that started the eagle-cam phenomenon — bald eagles streaming from a cottonwood above a trout hatchery in small-town Iowa since 2011, long before live nature cams were everywhere.
locations.fun_fact: The original Decorah cam once drew over 200 million views in a single nesting season.

locations.name: Tembe Elephant Park
locations.blurb: Rare sand forest on the Mozambique border, home to some of Africa's last great 'tusker' elephants — bulls whose ivory nearly sweeps the ground. The waterhole cam here is one of the longest-running in Africa.
locations.fun_fact: Tembe's elephants carry some of the best big-tusk genes left on the continent — trophy hunting never reached this population.

locations.name: Djuma Waterhole
locations.blurb: A waterhole in the Sabi Sand, the private reserves stitched to Kruger's unfenced western flank — the best leopard country in Africa. Djuma ran the world's first live safari webcam, and the waterhole has streamed ever since.
locations.fun_fact: Djuma's original webcam went live in 1998 — arguably the first wildlife live cam on the internet.

locations.name: GRACE Gorilla Forest
locations.blurb: The world's only sanctuary for rescued Grauer's gorillas, in the green hills of eastern DR Congo. The cam looks into the forest corridor where orphaned gorillas relearn how to be wild, in the care of an all-Congolese team.
locations.fun_fact: Grauer's gorilla is the largest gorilla subspecies on Earth — and lives only in eastern DR Congo.

locations.name: Lola ya Bonobo
locations.blurb: 'Paradise for bonobos' — the world's only bonobo sanctuary, in forest on the edge of Kinshasa. Bonobos exist nowhere on Earth but DR Congo, south of the great bend of the Congo River.
locations.fun_fact: Bonobos share about 98.7% of their DNA with humans — the same as chimpanzees, our two closest living relatives.

locations.name: Mpala · Laikipia
locations.blurb: A working research savanna on Kenya's Laikipia plateau, where the science of African wildlife actually gets done. Two cams: a watering hole on the plains and a river bend where hippos and crocs idle all day.
locations.fun_fact: Mpala's 48,000 acres host one of the highest wildlife densities in East Africa outside any national park.

locations.name: Amboseli · Kilimanjaro View
locations.blurb: A waterhole on Amboseli's edge with Mount Kilimanjaro filling the sky behind it — big-tusked elephants against Africa's highest mountain, the postcard that made Kenya famous, live.
locations.fun_fact: Amboseli's elephants are the most studied on Earth — researchers have tracked every individual family here since 1972.

locations.name: ol Donyo · Chyulu Hills
locations.blurb: A lodge waterhole in the young volcanic Chyulu Hills, between Amboseli and Tsavo — famous for 'super tusker' elephants that drink close enough for the mic to catch them breathing.
locations.fun_fact: The Chyulu Hills are geological infants — parts of the range erupted into existence only a few hundred years ago.

locations.name: Okaukuejo · Etosha
locations.blurb: Etosha's floodlit waterhole is the classic night stage of African wildlife: elephants, lions, and — the real prize — black rhino stepping out of the dark to drink. After sunset is prime time.
locations.fun_fact: Etosha's waterholes host one of the world's largest remaining black rhino populations — best seen at exactly this spot, at night.

locations.name: Namib Desert Waterhole
locations.blurb: A waterhole set against the fossil dunes of the world's oldest desert — oryx, springbok, ostrich and jackals drifting in and out of apricot-coloured sand, sunrise to starlight.
locations.fun_fact: The Namib has been arid for some 55 million years — its dunes glow orange because the sand has had time to rust.

locations.name: Victoria Falls Waterhole
locations.blurb: A busy waterhole minutes from Victoria Falls, with a twist: a daily 'vulture restaurant' feeding that turns the sky into a spiralling tower of hundreds of birds around midday.
locations.fun_fact: The lodge's daily vulture feeding is a conservation programme — supplementary meals keep endangered vultures away from poisoned carcasses.

locations.name: Chengdu Panda Base
locations.blurb: Giant pandas doing very little, magnificently — a 24/7 feed from the world's leading panda research and breeding base on the edge of Chengdu, Sichuan.
locations.fun_fact: A giant panda spends 10 to 16 hours a day eating bamboo — the cam rarely lacks action, if chewing counts.
